name: Circle Cliffs
short_description: Public BLM land in south-central Utah offering agate, chalcedony, and jasper collecting in colorful badlands terrain. Free rockhounding with 25 lb daily limits.
description:
Circle Cliffs is a scenic BLM rockhounding area in Garfield County, Utah, located within the colorful badlands and plateau country of south-central Utah. This remote public land site offers rockhounders the opportunity to collect agate, chalcedony, jasper, and petrified wood fragments in a geologically rich landscape. The area sits within the Circle Cliffs structural basin, part of the larger Colorado Plateau province, where ancient lacustrine and fluvial deposits have weathered to expose agates and chalcedonic materials.

The terrain at Circle Cliffs is characterized by eroded badlands, colorful clay and siltstone hills, and scattered washes that have naturally concentrated collectible specimens. Collectors will find specimens weathering from hillsides and concentrated in wash beds where water erosion has concentrated lighter materials. The landscape offers excellent opportunities for surface collecting without the need for heavy tools or digging.

As BLM public land, Circle Cliffs allows free recreational rockhounding under standard BLM personal-use guidelines. This location is ideal for rockhounders seeking remote, undeveloped collecting with minimal crowds. The colorful geology and accessibility to specimens make it appealing for both beginners and experienced collectors. The area is best visited during cooler months when hiking and collecting is comfortable in this high-elevation desert environment.

Visitors should come prepared for remote conditions, limited services, and weather variability. Cell service is spotty to nonexistent in this remote region. The nearest substantial services are in towns over 30 miles away, so plan accordingly with water, fuel, and supplies before traveling here.
history: Circle Cliffs sits within the Cretaceous-Tertiary lacustrine and fluvial sedimentary deposits of the Colorado Plateau. The colorful badlands formed from the erosion of clay-rich paleocene and eocene mudstones and siltstones, with agates and chalcedony precipitating from silica-rich groundwater during diagenesis. This region was prospected during Utah's early mining era, though the softer deposits held less economic interest than hard-rock mineral zones elsewhere in the state.
tips: Search wash bottoms and bajada fans where water erosion has concentrated lighter agate and chalcedony material over time - these areas often yield the best specimens with minimal effort. Look for colorful translucent to opaque pieces weathering from hillsides; jasper tends toward brick-red and brown tones, while chalcedony ranges from white to gray. Bring a small hand rake or classifier to sift wash gravels efficiently, and focus on areas where previous collectors have worked - these spots indicate productive zones. Early morning collecting and late afternoon light help spot colorful specimens against the tan and gray badlands backdrop.
terrain_notes: Circle Cliffs sits at approximately 5,200-5,600 feet elevation in high-desert badlands terrain with colorful eroded hills, scattered vegetation (blackbrush, desert scrub), and sandy/gravelly washes. Terrain is rolling with moderate slopes; footing varies from firm gravelly areas to loose badland clay. Walking distance from parking to productive collecting is typically 0.5 to 2 miles across exposed badlands. No maintained trails; navigation is cross-country on open BLM land.
best_season: October - April. Circle Cliffs sits at moderate-high elevation where summer heat (May-September) can exceed 85-90 degrees F, making midday collecting uncomfortable. Fall and spring offer ideal temperatures for extended hiking and collecting. Winter is possible but occasional snow at this elevation may limit access.
written_directions: From Boulder, Utah (the nearest town with services), take Utah State Route 12 south toward Escalante for approximately 25 miles. Look for BLM access points and informal pullouts near the Circle Cliffs badlands formation on both sides of the highway. GPS navigation to 37.996224, -111.129089 will bring you directly to productive collecting terrain. High-clearance vehicles recommended; passenger vehicles can reach some pullouts but accessing remote interior collecting areas requires 4x4 or hiking from highway access.
road_conditions: Utah State Route 12 is paved and well-maintained. Access roads into badlands vary from rough dirt/gravel two-track to sandy washes; high-clearance vehicle recommended for remote interior access. Some pullouts accessible by standard passenger vehicles.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts and parking areas along Utah Route 12 and adjacent dirt roads. No developed parking facilities. Multiple dispersed parking areas; typical capacity 2-5 vehicles per pullout. Parking is free and unrestricted on BLM land.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA). No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or heavy equipment. Hand tools and small-scale surface collecting only. Respect public land and leave no large disturbances.
rules:
Personal use collection only
Maximum 25 pounds per day per person
No commercial collection permitted
No motorized excavation or heavy equipment
Hand tools and surface collecting only
No large-scale disturbance of terrain
Leave gates as you find them
nearest_services: Boulder, Utah (approximately 25 miles north) has gasoline, food, limited lodging, and supplies. Escalante, Utah (approximately 35 miles south) offers similar services. Plan for remote area conditions; bring sufficient water and supplies before departing paved highway.
accessibility_notes: Moderate terrain difficulty with uneven badlands topography, loose clay footing, and sandy wash beds. Not wheelchair accessible. Sturdy hiking boots strongly recommended due to loose material and potential for ankle twists on uneven ground. Shade is minimal; sun protection essential. Route-finding requires map or GPS navigation as there are no marked trails. Typical collecting hikes range 1-3 miles from parking.

faq.question: What gems can I find rockhounding at Circle Cliffs in Utah?
faq.answer: Circle Cliffs is known for agate, chalcedony, jasper, and petrified wood fragments. Agate tends toward translucent white, gray, and banded varieties, while jasper appears in brick-red and brown tones. Chalcedony ranges from opaque white to pale gray. These specimens weather from badlands hillsides and are concentrated in wash beds.

faq.question: Is rockhounding at Circle Cliffs free and do I need a permit?
faq.answer: Yes, rockhounding at Circle Cliffs is completely free. As BLM public land, no permit is required for recreational collecting. You simply need to follow the 25 lb per day personal-use limit under federal guidelines. This makes Circle Cliffs one of Utah's most accessible and affordable rockhounding destinations.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at Circle Cliffs?
faq.answer: October through April is ideal. Summer heat at this 5,200+ foot elevation exceeds 85-90 degrees, making daytime collecting uncomfortable. Fall and spring offer pleasant temperatures for extended hiking and collecting. Winter is possible but occasional snow may impact access.

faq.question: How difficult is collecting at Circle Cliffs for beginners?
faq.answer: Circle Cliffs is beginner-friendly for surface collecting. No digging or heavy tools are needed; specimens weather naturally from hillsides and washes. Terrain is moderate - uneven badlands require sturdy boots and navigation skills - but kids age 8+ can participate with adult supervision. Start near highway access areas for easier collecting.

faq.question: What tools and supplies do I need for rockhounding at Circle Cliffs?
faq.answer: Bring sturdy hiking boots, sun protection (hat, sunscreen), and 2-3 liters of water per person for this remote area. A small hand rake, classifier, or garden claw helps sift wash gravels. A GPS device or map/compass is essential for navigation. No powered tools are allowed - collecting must be by hand only.

faq.question: How remote is Circle Cliffs and what services are nearby?
faq.answer: Circle Cliffs is remote - cell service is spotty and unreliable. The nearest town with gas, food, and water is Boulder, Utah, about 25 miles north. Plan ahead with a full fuel tank, water, and supplies before heading into this BLM backcountry. There are no developed facilities on site.
